items.question: Who cares about generalization in a supervised ML project?
items.answer: Data scientists estimate it during development; ML engineers and product owners depend on it at deployment because users receive predictions on inputs the model never trained on — offline generalization proxies predict live usefulness.

items.question: What is generalization in supervised learning?
items.answer: Generalization is strong performance on unseen (x, y) drawn from the same problem — the model predicts accurately on new examples it was not fit on, rather than memorizing the training set.

items.question: When should you measure generalization instead of training error?
items.answer: Before every deployment decision, hyperparameter comparison, and model sign-off — whenever you need to know how the model will behave on future or held-out data, not on rows already used in fit().

items.question: Where in the workflow is generalization estimated?
items.answer: On held-out validation or test splits and in cross-validation folds — after fit() on training data only, using predict() on rows excluded from training, then comparing predictions to known labels for metrics.

items.question: Why is training accuracy alone misleading for generalization?
items.answer: Training metrics measure fit on seen data; flexible models can memorize noise and still achieve near-perfect training scores while failing on unseen examples — so training accuracy overstates real-world performance when overfitting occurs.

items.question: How do you quantify poor generalization caused by overfitting?
items.answer: Compute train and test error after a single fit on training data; a large generalization gap (test error much higher than train error) with low train error indicates the model memorized training specifics instead of learning transferable patterns.

items.question: Training error vs test error — what does each measure?
items.answer: Training error measures how well θ fits data used in fit() — it can be driven down by memorization. Test error on held-out labeled data estimates generalization to unseen examples and is the honest pre-deployment performance proxy.

items.question: Memorization vs generalization — how do metrics differ?
items.answer: Memorization yields very low training error but high test error (large generalization gap). Generalization yields low error on both training and held-out test data because learned patterns transfer to new examples from the same problem.

items.question: Cause: model is fit on validation rows before reporting 'validation accuracy'. Effect?
items.answer: Validation data is no longer unseen — reported metrics measure fit on partially seen data, not generalization, and scores are optimistically biased compared to true held-out performance.

items.question: Cause: deployment traffic shifts (new region, new product line) while the model stays unchanged. Effect?
items.answer: The i.i.d. assumption breaks — offline generalization estimates no longer predict live performance, and error rates rise even though training and historical test metrics looked strong.
